summary: The notebook implements a residual learning baseline for the NFL Big Data Bowl, predicting player positions by combining a kinematic constant-acceleration baseline with CatBoost models trained on residuals. It introduces role-specific heads for Targeted Receivers and Defensive Coverage players, blends them with a global model, and incorporates physics-based features, sequence lags, formation context, and a lightweight graph-based neighbor embedding. Readers should understand how residual learning stabilizes predictions and simplifies the regression task, how role-aware ensembling captures distinct movement patterns without degrading global performance, and how multi-GPU CatBoost can be enabled with minimal code changes. The approach demonstrates a practical, computationally efficient recipe for spatial tracking prediction.
competition_flow: Loads weekly NFL tracking data, engineers physics, sequence, formation, and graph-based neighbor features, trains CatBoost models on position residuals relative to a kinematic baseline using group-kfold cross-validation, blends global and role-specific predictions, and generates a submission file.
data_reading: Reads train/input_2023_w{week_num:02d}.csv and train/output_2023_w{week_num:02d}.csv for weeks 1-18 using pd.read_csv, parallelized across 18 weeks with multiprocessing.Pool, then concatenated.
Test data loaded via pd.read_csv from test_input.csv and test.csv.
data_processing: Converts player height to inches, computes BMI, derives heading/velocity/acceleration vectors from speed and direction.
Calculates ball-relative geometry, team formation centroids, relative coordinates, and bearing to ball.
Applies lagged features (1-5 frames), rolling mean/std (windows 3,5), and velocity deltas.
Computes weighted k-nearest neighbor embeddings based on distance and ally/opponent status.
Handles missing values with defaults/forward fills, replaces infinities with NaNs before filling with 0, and clips final predictions to field bounds.
features_engineering: Height/BMI, heading/velocity/acceleration vectors, ball-frame projections (parallel/perpendicular components).
Physics features (momentum, kinetic energy), role/side binary flags.
Team centroid/width/length, relative coordinates, bearing to ball.
Lagged features (1-5 frames), rolling mean/std (3,5 frames), velocity deltas.
Graph-based neighbor embeddings (weighted ally/opp distance, velocity diffs, counts, min distances).
cv_strategy: GroupKFold(n_splits=5) grouped by game_id_play_id_nfl_id to prevent data leakage across frames of the same play/player.
ensembling: Averages predictions across 5 folds per head, then blends global and role-specific (Targeted Receiver, Defensive Coverage) predictions using fixed 0.5 weights before adding the kinematic baseline back and clipping to field bounds.
insights: Residual learning with a kinematic baseline stabilizes position predictions and simplifies the regression task for tree models.
Role-specific heads capture distinct movement patterns for key player types without degrading global performance.
Lightweight graph-based neighbor embeddings effectively encode spatial context without requiring heavy GNN frameworks.
Multi-GPU CatBoost can be enabled with minimal code changes by passing detected device IDs directly to the devices parameter.
